item.title: A Bar at the Folies-Bergère
item.lesson: Manet's last major painting, finished a year before his death, is built around a mirror that refuses to behave. A barmaid — a real one, named Suzon, who posed in his studio — stands behind her marble counter at Paris's most glittering nightspot, gazing out with an expression nobody has ever pinned down: boredom, melancholy, professional blankness, all of them. Behind her, the mirror shows the crowd, the trapeze artist's green slippers in the top corner — and her own reflection, shifted impossibly to the right, in conversation with a top-hatted customer who, by the geometry, should be standing exactly where you are. Generations of scholars have measured the optics; the consensus is that Manet knew precisely what he was doing. The painting splits her in two — the public transaction in the mirror, the private self facing us — and in doing so states the great modern theme: being surrounded and alone at once. The bottles and mandarins up front are as gorgeous as anything he ever painted.
item.lookFor: Check the beer bottles: red triangles mark them as Bass ale, English — a wry note on the cosmopolitan crowd. Then try to reconcile her reflection with her pose. You can't. That seam is the painting's true subject.
